text:
While the oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is a cornerstone of modern catalytic cross-coupling, a comprehensive mechanistic understanding of this fundamental step has remained elusive. In this study, we employ a combination of electroanalytical and statistical modeling techniques to interrogate the electronic and steric drivers of Ni(I) reactivity. By utilizing cyclic voltammetry for the rapid in situ generation of four classes of catalytically relevant Ni(I) species—supported by MeBPy, MePhen, Terpy, and BPP ligands—we measured more than 200 experimental oxidative addition rates across a diverse library of aryl iodide substrates. 

Integrating this kinetic data into multivariate linear regression models allowed us to classify the reaction mechanisms based on ligand architecture. Our findings reveal a mechanistic divergence: while complexes supported by bipyridine, phenanthroline, and terpyridine ligands typically proceed via a three-center concerted pathway, those featuring the BPP ligand favor a halogen-atom abstraction mechanism. To broaden the utility of these insights, we developed a global heat map of predicted oxidative addition rates for over 2,000 commercially available aryl iodides. Finally, we demonstrate the practical relevance of our models by correlating predicted kinetic trends with the reaction outcomes of a reported Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling, providing a quantitative framework for understanding and optimizing nickel-mediated catalysis.
